0:99214.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
0:99214.citation: Use CareGap's CMS PFS pricing tool to compare each CPT code's charge against the Medicare-allowed amount. A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
0:99214.why_it_applies: The $245.00 charge for CPT 99214 is 2.9x the Medicare-allowed rate of $84.20, approaching the threshold the KB identifies as potentially excessive, giving the patient grounds to formally dispute this charge through the provider's billing department or their insurer.

1:73560.title: Common Billing Errors to Check
1:73560.citation: A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
1:73560.why_it_applies: CPT 73560 is billed at $180.00 against a Medicare rate of $34.28, representing a 5.3x multiplier that clearly exceeds the 3-5x threshold flagged as potentially excessive in the knowledge base.

1:73560.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
1:73560.citation: I've reviewed my itemized bill and CPT code [XXXXX] appears to be [upcoded/duplicated/higher than the fair market rate]. Can you review this charge?
1:73560.why_it_applies: The 5.3x overcharge on CPT 73560 gives the patient clear grounds to contact Garden State Orthopedic Specialists' billing department and formally dispute the charge as significantly above fair market rate, following the step-by-step process outlined in this section.

1:73560.title: Key Federal Agencies & Resources
1:73560.citation: State Insurance Commissioner | Insurance disputes | [varies by state]
1:73560.why_it_applies: Since the patient has BlueCross PPO coverage and the provider is in New Jersey, the patient can escalate an unresolved overcharge dispute to the New Jersey State Insurance Commissioner or leverage New Jersey's strong Out-of-Network Consumer Protection Act protections.

2:73562.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
2:73562.citation: Use CareGap's CMS PFS pricing tool to compare each CPT code's charge against the Medicare-allowed amount. A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
2:73562.why_it_applies: At 5.3x the Medicare rate, this charge explicitly exceeds the 3-5x threshold flagged as potentially excessive in the dispute guidance, giving the patient a clear basis to formally dispute CPT 73562 with the provider's billing department.

3:73564.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
3:73564.citation: A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
3:73564.why_it_applies: At 5.9x the Medicare rate, this charge exceeds the knowledge base's own benchmark of 3-5x as potentially excessive, giving the patient clear grounds to formally dispute CPT 73564 through the provider's billing department and, if unresolved, through their insurer or state regulators.

3:73564.title: State-Level Protections (Selected States)
3:73564.citation: New Jersey — Out-of-Network Consumer Protection Act: Among the strongest state protections; limits patient liability for inadvertent out-of-network care.
3:73564.why_it_applies: The provider is Garden State Orthopedic Specialists, suggesting a New Jersey location; if any portion of this visit involved out-of-network billing, New Jersey's Out-of-Network Consumer Protection Act may provide additional leverage in disputing the excessive charge beyond federal protections.

4:20610.title: How to Dispute a Medical Bill — Step-by-Step
4:20610.citation: Use CareGap's CMS PFS pricing tool to compare each CPT code's charge against the Medicare-allowed amount. A charge significantly above the Medicare rate (e.g., 3-5x or more) may be excessive.
4:20610.why_it_applies: At 9.7x the Medicare-allowed rate, this charge far exceeds the 3-5x threshold the KB identifies as potentially excessive, giving the patient clear grounds to formally dispute CPT 20610 through the provider's billing department and, if unresolved, through their insurer or state regulators.

letter.subject: Formal Dispute of Medical Bill — Account No. 612847 — Sarah L. Thompson — Date of Service 04/02/2024
letter.body:
Sarah L. Thompson
[Your Address]

April 2, 2025

Garden State Orthopedic Specialists
800 Nassau St, Suite 210
Princeton, NJ 08542

Re: Formal Billing Dispute
Patient Name: Sarah L. Thompson
Date of Service: April 2, 2024
Account Number: 612847
Insurer: BlueCross PPO
Total Amount Billed: $1,730.00

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing on behalf of patient Sarah L. Thompson to formally dispute multiple charges appearing on the bill associated with Account Number 612847, for services rendered on April 2, 2024. After conducting a thorough review of the itemized statement and comparing each billed procedure against the applicable Medicare Physician Fee Schedule (PFS) allowable rates published by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS), I have identified five line items that appear to reflect charges significantly in excess of established benchmarks. These discrepancies raise serious concerns about billing accuracy and fair pricing.

I respectfully request that your billing department review each of the charges identified below, provide a written explanation for the basis of each charge, and issue corrected invoices where appropriate.

DISPUTED CHARGES

1. CPT 99214 — Office Visit, Level 4 (Moderate Complexity)
   Amount Billed: $245.00
   Medicare Allowable Rate: $84.20
   Multiplier: 2.9x the Medicare rate
   This charge exceeds the Medicare-allowed amount by approximately 191 percent. A charge at this level warrants a detailed explanation of the medical necessity and documentation supporting a Level 4 complexity designation.

2. CPT 73560 — X-Ray, Knee, 1-2 Views
   Amount Billed: $180.00
   Medicare Allowable Rate: $34.28
   Multiplier: 5.3x the Medicare rate
   This charge exceeds the Medicare-allowed amount by approximately 425 percent. A charge at this magnitude is substantially above what is considered a reasonable and customary rate for this service.

3. CPT 73562 — X-Ray, Knee, 3 Views
   Amount Billed: $225.00
   Medicare Allowable Rate: $42.27
   Multiplier: 5.3x the Medicare rate
   This charge exceeds the Medicare-allowed amount by approximately 432 percent. I request documentation confirming that three separate views were medically necessary and were not duplicative of the views captured under CPT 73560 or CPT 73564.

4. CPT 73564 — X-Ray, Knee, Complete (4 or More Views)
   Amount Billed: $290.00
   Medicare Allowable Rate: $49.25
   Multiplier: 5.9x the Medicare rate
   This charge exceeds the Medicare-allowed amount by approximately 489 percent. I also note that CPT codes 73560, 73562, and 73564 have all been billed on the same date of service. Billing multiple knee X-ray codes together on the same date for the same patient may constitute duplicate billing or unbundling, which is inconsistent with CMS coding guidelines. I request a full explanation of the clinical justification for billing all three codes simultaneously.

5. CPT 20610 — Arthrocentesis or Joint Injection, Major Joint
   Amount Billed: $385.00
   Medicare Allowable Rate: $39.60
   Multiplier: 9.7x the Medicare rate
   This charge exceeds the Medicare-allowed amount by approximately 872 percent. This is the most significant discrepancy on the bill and requires immediate review and written justification.

TOTAL DISPUTED AMOUNT: $1,325.00 (representing the five line items identified above out of the total billed amount of $1,730.00)

APPLICABLE LEGAL FRAMEWORK

This dispute is submitted with reference to the following applicable laws and regulations:

No Surprises Act (effective January 1, 2022): This federal law establishes protections against unexpected medical bills and requires that patients receive good-faith cost estimates for scheduled services. To the extent any of these charges were not disclosed in advance, they may be subject to challenge under this Act.

HIPAA Privacy Rule, 45 C.F.R. Section 164.524: Under this provision, I formally request a complete copy of the itemized bill, including all CPT codes, revenue codes, diagnosis codes, and any supporting clinical documentation used to determine the level of service billed. This request should be fulfilled within 30 days as required by law.

New Jersey Out-of-Network Consumer Protection Act: New Jersey provides among the strongest state-level consumer protections regarding out-of-network billing. To the extent any provider or service involved in this visit was out of network, patient liability may be limited under this statute. I request written confirmation of the network status of each provider and service reflected on this bill.

CMS Physician Fee Schedule Benchmarks: While Medicare rates do not directly govern private billing, they represent a widely accepted standard for evaluating the reasonableness of medical charges. Charges that are 3 to 5 times or more above the Medicare-allowed amount are broadly recognized as potentially excessive and subject to dispute.

REQUESTED ACTIONS

I respectfully request that Garden State Orthopedic Specialists take the following actions within 30 days of receipt of this letter:

1. Provide a complete itemized bill for Account Number 612847, including all CPT codes, modifiers, diagnosis codes, units billed, and the charge for each line item, as required under HIPAA Section 164.524.

2. Provide written justification for each of the five disputed charges identified above, including the clinical documentation and coding rationale supporting the billed amounts.

3. Provide a written explanation for the simultaneous billing of CPT codes 73560, 73562, and 73564 on the same date of service for the same patient.

4. Issue a corrected statement reflecting any adjustments determined to be appropriate following your internal review.

5. Confirm in writing the network status of each provider involved in the April 2, 2024 visit relative to the patient's BlueCross PPO plan.

Please direct all correspondence related to this dispute to the patient, Sarah L. Thompson, at the address listed above, or to the authorized advocate acting on her behalf.

NEXT STEPS IF UNRESOLVED

If I do not receive a substantive written response within 30 days of the date of this letter, or if the disputed charges are not adequately addressed, I will pursue the following remedies without further notice:

1. Filing a formal complaint with the New Jersey Division of Consumer Affairs and the New Jersey Department of Banking and Insurance.

2. Filing a complaint with the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB) regarding unfair or deceptive billing practices.

3. Submitting a complaint to the New Jersey Attorney General's Office regarding potential violations of state consumer protection statutes.

4. Escalating the matter to BlueCross PPO for a formal insurance-level billing dispute review.

I want to emphasize that this letter is submitted in good faith and with the sincere expectation that this matter can be resolved through direct communication. I remain open to a prompt and cooperative resolution.

Sincerely,

Authorized Patient Advocate
On Behalf of Sarah L. Thompson
Account Number: 612847
Date of Service: April 2, 2024
letter.recipient_block:
To:
Garden State Orthopedic Specialists
800 Nassau St, Suite 210
Princeton, NJ 08542

roadmap.title: Request Itemized Bill with CPT Codes
roadmap.detail: Contact Garden State Orthopedic Specialists billing department and request a fully itemized bill listing every service, procedure code (CPT), and charge. You are legally entitled to this document and it is the foundation for disputing any errors.

roadmap.title: Review Flagged Charges Against Benchmark Rates
roadmap.detail: CareGap has already identified 5 flagged charges with potential savings of $1,075.40 on your $1,730.00 bill. Cross-reference each flagged line item on your itemized bill against the CPT codes and amounts identified in your dispute analysis to confirm discrepancies before writing your letter.

roadmap.title: Send a Formal Dispute Letter to the Billing Department
roadmap.detail: Write a certified dispute letter to Garden State Orthopedic Specialists referencing each of the 5 flagged charges by CPT code, stating the amount billed versus the reasonable benchmark rate, and requesting a corrected bill or written justification. Send via certified mail with return receipt so you have a documented record.

roadmap.title: File a Grievance with Your Insurance Provider
roadmap.detail: If you are insured, submit a formal grievance to your health plan citing the overbilled charges and attach your dispute letter and itemized bill. Insurers are required to respond to grievances within defined timeframes and may negotiate directly with the provider on your behalf.

roadmap.title: File a Complaint with the New Jersey Insurance Commissioner or Attorney General
roadmap.detail: If the provider or insurer does not resolve the dispute satisfactorily, file a complaint with the New Jersey Department of Banking and Insurance or the NJ Attorney General's office. New Jersey has state-level billing protections that may apply to your situation and regulators can compel a response.

roadmap.title: Consult a Healthcare Billing Advocate or Attorney
roadmap.detail: With $1,075.40 in potential savings at stake, engaging a certified medical billing advocate or a healthcare attorney is strongly warranted if prior steps have not produced results. Many advocates work on contingency or a percentage of savings recovered, making this a low-risk option for a dispute of this size.

summary:
# Your Medical Bill Summary

You were charged $245 for an office visit, but Medicare says that same visit should only cost $84.20—that's a difference of about $161 per visit. Overall, this bill has about $1,075 in charges that look way higher than they should be out of a $1,730 total. It's worth calling the office to ask why they charged so much more than the standard rate, or getting a second opinion from your insurance company about whether you should pay the full amount.
